value.claim: A large, deep convolutional neural network with 60 million parameters achieves top-1 and top-5 error rates of 37.5% and 17.0% on the ILSVRC-2010 test set, considerably better than the previous state-of-the-art.
A variant of the model entered in the ILSVRC-2012 competition achieved a winning top-5 test error rate of 15.3%, compared to 26.2% achieved by the second-best entry.
Deep convolutional neural networks with ReLU non-linearities train several times faster than their equivalents with saturating neurons (e.g., tanh).
Local response normalization, which implements a form of lateral inhibition, reduces top-1 and top-5 error rates by 1.4% and 1.2% respectively.
Overlapping pooling (with stride 2 and neighborhood size 3) reduces top-1 and top-5 error rates by 0.4% and 0.3% respectively compared to traditional non-overlapping pooling.

The depth of the network is important for performance; removing any of the five convolutional layers results in inferior performance, with middle layer removal causing about a 2% loss in top-1 performance.
A highly-optimized GPU implementation of 2D convolution and other CNN training operations was written and made publicly available.
